questions.question: What is the name given to the 40-day period leading up to Easter?
questions.explanation: Lent is the 40 days before Easter, a time when Christians historically fasted or gave up certain foods.

questions.question: The official Union Flag consists of the crosses of three countries. Which UK nation's flag is NOT represented in the Union Flag?
questions.explanation: The Welsh dragon does not appear on the Union Flag because, at the time the first flag was created in 1606, Wales was already united with England.

questions.question: What is the primary responsibility of the judiciary in the UK?
options: To interpret the law and ensure fair trials
questions.explanation: Judges collectively form 'the judiciary.' Their role is to interpret the laws passed by Parliament and ensure court trials are conducted fairly.

questions.question: In what year was the Habeas Corpus Act passed into law?
questions.explanation: Passed in 1679, the Habeas Corpus Act guaranteed that no one could be held as a prisoner unlawfully without a court hearing.

questions.question: What term is used to describe the sustained German bombing campaigns on UK cities during the Second World War?
questions.explanation: The Blitz refers to the night-time bombing of London and other British cities by the German air force. The term comes from the German word 'Blitzkrieg', meaning lightning war.

questions.question: Why is the historical event of 1688 referred to as the 'Glorious Revolution'?
options: Because it granted voting rights to women
Because it resulted in major technological achievements
Because the monarchy was restored with no armed conflict
Because the Spanish Armada was defeated
questions.explanation: It was called the Glorious Revolution because King James II fled to France and William of Orange took the throne without any fighting or bloodshed in England.

questions.question: Which of the following famous landmarks is situated in Greenwich, London?
questions.explanation: The O2 is a large entertainment venue located on the Greenwich Peninsula in South East London.

questions.question: Following the national referendum in 2016, in what year did the United Kingdom officially leave the European Union?
questions.explanation: The UK officially left the European Union on January 31, 2020, following the Brexit vote held in 2016.

questions.question: Sir Robert Walpole is widely recognized as the first British Prime Minister. During which period was he in power?
questions.explanation: Sir Robert Walpole served as Prime Minister from 1721 to 1742, making him the longest-serving Prime Minister in British history.

questions.question: Which nation within the UK has the thistle as its national flower?
questions.explanation: The thistle is the national flower of Scotland. England's is the rose, Wales has the daffodil, and Northern Ireland has the shamrock.

questions.question: Which 18th-century Scottish thinker developed highly influential economic ideas during the Enlightenment?
questions.explanation: Adam Smith was a key figure during the Scottish Enlightenment who developed ideas about economics that form the basis of modern capitalism.

questions.question: If you rent a single room in a shared house and want to watch live television in your room, do you need your own individual TV licence?
options: True - you must buy a separate licence.
False - one licence covers the entire property regardless of rental agreements.
questions.explanation: While one licence covers an entire address for a single household, people renting individual rooms under separate tenancy agreements must each have their own TV licence.

questions.question: Justices of the Peace (JPs) and Magistrates are typically drawn from which group?
questions.explanation: Magistrates and JPs are unpaid members of the local community who decide minor cases in their local areas. They do not need legal qualifications.

questions.question: A two-minute silence is traditionally observed on Easter Sunday to honor the death of Jesus Christ.
questions.explanation: False. A two-minute silence is observed on Remembrance Day (Armistice Day) in November to commemorate those who died in wars, not on Easter.

questions.question: How long do 'Life Peers' serve in the House of Lords once nominated?
options: Until the Prime Minister who nominated them resigns
questions.explanation: Life Peers are appointed for their lifetime and their title cannot be inherited by their children. They are usually nominated by the Prime Minister.

questions.question: The Battle of Agincourt was a famous military conflict fought between which two nations?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Agincourt took place in 1415. King Henry V of England famously defeated the French army despite being heavily outnumbered.

questions.question: Which prominent figure is credited with establishing the National Health Service (NHS) in the UK in 1948?
questions.explanation: Aneurin (Nye) Bevan, the Minister of Health in the post-war Labour government, spearheaded the creation of the NHS to provide healthcare based on need, free at the point of use.

questions.question: The Speaker of the House of Commons is a standard Member of Parliament (MP) who represents a constituency and deals with local issues.
questions.explanation: True. The Speaker remains an MP and represents their local constituency's issues, even though their primary role is to neutrally chair the debates in the House of Commons.

questions.question: During the 16th and 17th centuries, the Puritans advocated for stricter religious practices. Which branch of religion did they follow?
questions.explanation: Puritans were strict Protestants who believed that the Church of England needed to be 'purified' of its remaining Catholic practices and rituals.

questions.question: Who holds the authority to nominate individuals to become Life Peers in the House of Lords?
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister nominates Life Peers. The Monarch officially grants the title, but this is done solely on the advice of the Prime Minister.

questions.question: The Second World War was triggered when Adolf Hitler ordered the invasion of which country in 1939?
questions.explanation: Britain and France declared war on Germany in September 1939 immediately after Hitler invaded Poland.

questions.question: What is the name of the famous recurring cricket series played exclusively between England and Australia?
questions.explanation: The Ashes is a legendary Test cricket series. The name originated from a satirical obituary stating that English cricket had died and 'the body will be cremated and the ashes taken to Australia'.

questions.question: In England and Wales, the 'small claims' court procedure is designed to settle minor financial disputes. What is the maximum claim amount allowed under this informal procedure?
questions.explanation: The small claims procedure in England and Wales handles claims of less than £10,000. It is an informal way to settle disputes without needing a lawyer.

questions.question: Following the departure of the Roman Empire in AD 410, which two tribal groups primarily invaded and settled in Britain?
questions.explanation: After the Romans left, Britain was invaded by tribes from northern Europe, primarily the Jutes, the Angles, and the Saxons (collectively known as the Anglo-Saxons).
